title: Warfarin — Initiation & Reversal
oneLiner: Vitamin K antagonist for thromboembolism prevention; requires safe initiation with INR monitoring and rapid reversal for bleeding or high INR with major bleed risk.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Intracranial haemorrhage:** sudden severe headache, focal neurology, depressed GCS; do NOT wait for INR before CT head if suspected.
- **Massive GI bleed:** haematemesis/melaena with hypotension, tachycardia; need immediate cross-match, IV access, and reversal.
- **Airway compromise from upper airway haematoma:** stridor, neck swelling (retropharyngeal/laryngeal); call anaesthetics NOW, give PCC while preparing surgical airway.
sections.Presentation:
- **Bleeding:** easy bruising, epistaxis, gum bleeding, haematuria, melaena, haematochezia, haemoptysis, prolonged bleeding from cuts.
- **Asymptomatic high INR (incidental lab finding)** often in clinic or pre-procedure.
- **Over-coagulation without bleeding:** fatigue, pallor from chronic blood loss.
- **Thrombotic complications of reversal:** rarely, rebound thrombosis after over-reversal (e.g. in mechanical valves).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Other coagulopathies:**
    - Liver disease (low albumin, prolonged PT/APTT, normal TT)
    - DIC (low platelets, elevated D-dimer, schistocytes on smear)
    - Vitamin K deficiency (malnutrition, obstructive jaundice)
- **Platelet dysfunction:** NSAIDs, uraemia, myelodysplasia (normal INR/APTT, prolonged bleeding time)
- **Underlying bleeding disorder:** haemophilia, von Willebrand disease (check factor levels)
- **Splitter:** bleeding with isolated prolonged INR (vs APTT + INR + platelets) → warfarin effect is most likely if known use.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
    - Point-of-care INR (e.g. CoaguChek) — immediate result, confirm with lab if uncertain.
    - Capillary glucose (hypoglycaemia mimics neurological symptoms).
    - Urine dipstick (if haematuria).
- **Bloods:**
    - Lab INR, APTT, FBC, U&E, LFTs, group & screen (if bleeding).
    - If bleeding: cross-match packed red cells (consider 4-6 units if massive).
- **Imaging:**
    - CT head (urgent, non-contrast) for any neurological symptom or trauma with head injury.
    - CT chest/abdomen/pelvis if haemodynamic instability or suspected retroperitoneal bleed (flank pain, back pain, falling Hb).
- **Never-miss test:**
    - **CT head in any patient on warfarin with acute headache, confusion, focal neurology, or seizure** — even with normal INR.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - Stop warfarin.
    - If life-threatening bleeding or need for emergency surgery:
        - Give Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (PCC) [dose per local policy, e.g. 25–50 units/kg] AND **Vitamin K 5 mg IV slow** (≥30 minutes) — PCC works instantly, vitamin K sustains reversal.
        - If PCC unavailable: Fresh Frozen Plasma (FFP) 15 mL/kg (risk of volume overload). Add IV vitamin K to block ongoing effect.
        - For critical haemorrhage: activate massive transfusion protocol (PRBCs:FFP:platelets 1:1:1).
    - If INR >8 without bleeding (asymptomatic high INR):
        - Hold warfarin.
        - Give Vitamin K 1–5 mg orally (or IV if NBM). Check INR 24h later.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
    - Reversal: recheck INR 6–12h after vitamin K ± PCC. If still high, repeat dose.
    - Initiation (if new warfarin start):
        - Use a loading nomogram (e.g. Fennerty protocol): start 5–10 mg daily for 2–3 days, then adjust per daily INR.
        - Check INR daily at same time before dosing.
        - Aim for therapeutic range (INR 2–3 for most indications; 2.5–3.5 for mechanical mitral valves or recurrent VTE).
        - After stable INR 2 consecutive days, reduce monitoring to 3–4 days, then weekly, then monthly.
    - Continue bridging heparin/LMWH until INR ≥2 (for VTE) or ≥2.5 (for mechanical valve) for at least 2 days.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - Regular INR monitoring in anticoagulation clinic.
    - Review diet (vitamin K intake changes), interacting drugs (amiodarone, antibiotics, antifungals, NSAIDs).
    - Patient education: carry anticoagulant card, report any bleeding.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - Fluids: maintenance IV fluids if bleeding or NBM (e.g. Hartmann’s 1 L/8h).
    - VTE prophylaxis: **do NOT give** if actively bleeding; resume after reversal and risk assessment.
    - Diet: normal unless GI bleeding; then NBM for endoscopy.
    - Observations: NEWS2 q15min if unstable, then hourly.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
    - **Initiation:** Warfarin 5 mg orally daily (starting dose; adjust per nomogram — see local protocol).
    - **Reversal (major bleeding):** Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (PCC) 25–50 units/kg IV (product-specific dosing per local policy) PLUS Vitamin K 5 mg IV (slow).
    - **Reversal (asymptomatic high INR):** Vitamin K 1–5 mg orally (or IV if NBM).
- **Common alternatives:**
    - FFP (15 mL/kg) if PCC unavailable.
    - For non-urgent reversal (INR 5–8, no bleeding): hold warfarin, consider low-dose vitamin K (1–2 mg oral); repeat INR next day.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
    - Avoid in pregnancy (except mechanical heart valves — use LMWH).
    - Major interaction: antibiotics (reduce gut flora, potentiate warfarin), amiodarone, NSAIDs (increase bleeding risk), enzyme inducers (rifampicin, carbamazepine) reduce effect.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
    - Renal impairment: no dose change for warfarin itself, but **renally cleared LMWH may accumulate** during bridging.
    - Hepatic impairment: baseline prolonged INR → start with lower dose (2.5–5 mg) and monitor carefully; reversal may require higher vitamin K.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
    - **Giving a routine warfarin dose without checking current INR** (risk of supratherapeutic INR and bleeds).
    - **Using only vitamin K (without PCC) in life-threatening bleeding** — effect takes 6–24h; patient may exsanguinate.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
    - Major bleeding (intracranial, GI, retroperitoneal, intraocular)
    - Warfarin-induced skin necrosis (protein C deficiency — seen in first few days, painful necrotic plaques)
    - Purple toes syndrome (cholesterol emboli from anticoagulation — rare)
- **Chronic:**
    - Osteoporosis (long-term use increased fracture risk)
    - Rebound thrombosis if warfarin stopped abruptly (especially mechanical valves)
sections.Prognosis: - Good if careful INR control (TTR >65%). Major bleeding mortality ~10–15%. Intracranial haemorrhage mortality >50%.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check INR before giving warfarin dose**, even if the patient "had it yesterday."
- **Use a warfarin initiation chart** (NICE-recommended) and prescribe daily INR with dose adjustment.
- **Give vitamin K orally for asymptomatic high INR (INR >8, no bleeding)** — avoid IV unless NBM; IV can cause anaphylactoid reaction if given too fast.
- **In life-threatening haemorrhage, remember to give PCC and vitamin K together** — PCC reverses INR immediately, vitamin K sustains reversal for 24h.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give FFP alone for urgent reversal** — FFP takes hours to correct INR and may cause volume overload. PCC is superior.
- **Do not restart warfarin without checking INR after reversal** — may re-bleed.
- **Do not discharge a patient after high INR reversal without arranging a repeat INR within 24–48h** — rebound thrombosis risk.
- **Do not omit bridging LMWH if warfarin is held >48h in a high-thrombotic-risk patient** (mechanical mitral valve, recent VTE, atrial fibrillation with prior stroke).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
    - New onset bleeding (black stool, blood in urine, coughing blood, severe headache)
    - Unexplained bruising or petechiae
    - Any fall or head injury (even if asymptomatic)
- **Follow-up:**
    - Anticoagulation clinic INR check **within 2–5 days** (sooner if reversal given)
    - GP review if new symptoms or medication changes
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
    - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially tachycardia, hypotension)
    - Suspected intracranial bleed (urgent CT head needed)
    - Haemodynamically unstable major haemorrhage (systolic BP <90, HR >120)
    - Lactate >4 mmol/L
    - GCS drop by ≥2 points
    - INR >10 with active bleeding (or need for emergency surgery) — call haematology on-call for PCC dosing
- **Crash team:**
    - Cardiac arrest (consider haemorrhagic shock, tamponade, tension pneumothorax)
    - Seizure (possibility of intracranial bleed)

rawMarkdown:
## Warfarin — Initiation & Reversal
**One-liner:** Vitamin K antagonist for thromboembolism prevention; requires safe initiation with INR monitoring and rapid reversal for bleeding or high INR with major bleed risk.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Intracranial haemorrhage:** sudden severe headache, focal neurology, depressed GCS; do NOT wait for INR before CT head if suspected.
- **Massive GI bleed:** haematemesis/melaena with hypotension, tachycardia; need immediate cross-match, IV access, and reversal.
- **Airway compromise from upper airway haematoma:** stridor, neck swelling (retropharyngeal/laryngeal); call anaesthetics NOW, give PCC while preparing surgical airway.

### Presentation
- **Bleeding:** easy bruising, epistaxis, gum bleeding, haematuria, melaena, haematochezia, haemoptysis, prolonged bleeding from cuts.
- **Asymptomatic high INR (incidental lab finding)** often in clinic or pre-procedure.
- **Over-coagulation without bleeding:** fatigue, pallor from chronic blood loss.
- **Thrombotic complications of reversal:** rarely, rebound thrombosis after over-reversal (e.g. in mechanical valves).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Other coagulopathies:**
    - Liver disease (low albumin, prolonged PT/APTT, normal TT)
    - DIC (low platelets, elevated D-dimer, schistocytes on smear)
    - Vitamin K deficiency (malnutrition, obstructive jaundice)
- **Platelet dysfunction:** NSAIDs, uraemia, myelodysplasia (normal INR/APTT, prolonged bleeding time)
- **Underlying bleeding disorder:** haemophilia, von Willebrand disease (check factor levels)
- **Splitter:** bleeding with isolated prolonged INR (vs APTT + INR + platelets) → warfarin effect is most likely if known use.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
    - Point-of-care INR (e.g. CoaguChek) — immediate result, confirm with lab if uncertain.
    - Capillary glucose (hypoglycaemia mimics neurological symptoms).
    - Urine dipstick (if haematuria).
- **Bloods:**
    - Lab INR, APTT, FBC, U&E, LFTs, group & screen (if bleeding).
    - If bleeding: cross-match packed red cells (consider 4-6 units if massive).
- **Imaging:**
    - CT head (urgent, non-contrast) for any neurological symptom or trauma with head injury.
    - CT chest/abdomen/pelvis if haemodynamic instability or suspected retroperitoneal bleed (flank pain, back pain, falling Hb).
- **Never-miss test:**
    - **CT head in any patient on warfarin with acute headache, confusion, focal neurology, or seizure** — even with normal INR.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - Stop warfarin.
    - If life-threatening bleeding or need for emergency surgery:
        - Give Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (PCC) [dose per local policy, e.g. 25–50 units/kg] AND **Vitamin K 5 mg IV slow** (≥30 minutes) — PCC works instantly, vitamin K sustains reversal.
        - If PCC unavailable: Fresh Frozen Plasma (FFP) 15 mL/kg (risk of volume overload). Add IV vitamin K to block ongoing effect.
        - For critical haemorrhage: activate massive transfusion protocol (PRBCs:FFP:platelets 1:1:1).
    - If INR >8 without bleeding (asymptomatic high INR):
        - Hold warfarin.
        - Give Vitamin K 1–5 mg orally (or IV if NBM). Check INR 24h later.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
    - Reversal: recheck INR 6–12h after vitamin K ± PCC. If still high, repeat dose.
    - Initiation (if new warfarin start):
        - Use a loading nomogram (e.g. Fennerty protocol): start 5–10 mg daily for 2–3 days, then adjust per daily INR.
        - Check INR daily at same time before dosing.
        - Aim for therapeutic range (INR 2–3 for most indications; 2.5–3.5 for mechanical mitral valves or recurrent VTE).
        - After stable INR 2 consecutive days, reduce monitoring to 3–4 days, then weekly, then monthly.
    - Continue bridging heparin/LMWH until INR ≥2 (for VTE) or ≥2.5 (for mechanical valve) for at least 2 days.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - Regular INR monitoring in anticoagulation clinic.
    - Review diet (vitamin K intake changes), interacting drugs (amiodarone, antibiotics, antifungals, NSAIDs).
    - Patient education: carry anticoagulant card, report any bleeding.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - Fluids: maintenance IV fluids if bleeding or NBM (e.g. Hartmann’s 1 L/8h).
    - VTE prophylaxis: **do NOT give** if actively bleeding; resume after reversal and risk assessment.
    - Diet: normal unless GI bleeding; then NBM for endoscopy.
    - Observations: NEWS2 q15min if unstable, then hourly.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
    - **Initiation:** Warfarin 5 mg orally daily (starting dose; adjust per nomogram — see local protocol).
    - **Reversal (major bleeding):** Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (PCC) 25–50 units/kg IV (product-specific dosing per local policy) PLUS Vitamin K 5 mg IV (slow).
    - **Reversal (asymptomatic high INR):** Vitamin K 1–5 mg orally (or IV if NBM).
- **Common alternatives:**
    - FFP (15 mL/kg) if PCC unavailable.
    - For non-urgent reversal (INR 5–8, no bleeding): hold warfarin, consider low-dose vitamin K (1–2 mg oral); repeat INR next day.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
    - Avoid in pregnancy (except mechanical heart valves — use LMWH).
    - Major interaction: antibiotics (reduce gut flora, potentiate warfarin), amiodarone, NSAIDs (increase bleeding risk), enzyme inducers (rifampicin, carbamazepine) reduce effect.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
    - Renal impairment: no dose change for warfarin itself, but **renally cleared LMWH may accumulate** during bridging.
    - Hepatic impairment: baseline prolonged INR → start with lower dose (2.5–5 mg) and monitor carefully; reversal may require higher vitamin K.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
    - **Giving a routine warfarin dose without checking current INR** (risk of supratherapeutic INR and bleeds).
    - **Using only vitamin K (without PCC) in life-threatening bleeding** — effect takes 6–24h; patient may exsanguinate.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
    - Major bleeding (intracranial, GI, retroperitoneal, intraocular)
    - Warfarin-induced skin necrosis (protein C deficiency — seen in first few days, painful necrotic plaques)
    - Purple toes syndrome (cholesterol emboli from anticoagulation — rare)
- **Chronic:**
    - Osteoporosis (long-term use increased fracture risk)
    - Rebound thrombosis if warfarin stopped abruptly (especially mechanical valves)

### Prognosis
- Good if careful INR control (TTR >65%). Major bleeding mortality ~10–15%. Intracranial haemorrhage mortality >50%.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check INR before giving warfarin dose**, even if the patient "had it yesterday."
- **Use a warfarin initiation chart** (NICE-recommended) and prescribe daily INR with dose adjustment.
- **Give vitamin K orally for asymptomatic high INR (INR >8, no bleeding)** — avoid IV unless NBM; IV can cause anaphylactoid reaction if given too fast.
- **In life-threatening haemorrhage, remember to give PCC and vitamin K together** — PCC reverses INR immediately, vitamin K sustains reversal for 24h.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give FFP alone for urgent reversal** — FFP takes hours to correct INR and may cause volume overload. PCC is superior.
- **Do not restart warfarin without checking INR after reversal** — may re-bleed.
- **Do not discharge a patient after high INR reversal without arranging a repeat INR within 24–48h** — rebound thrombosis risk.
- **Do not omit bridging LMWH if warfarin is held >48h in a high-thrombotic-risk patient** (mechanical mitral valve, recent VTE, atrial fibrillation with prior stroke).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
    - New onset bleeding (black stool, blood in urine, coughing blood, severe headache)
    - Unexplained bruising or petechiae
    - Any fall or head injury (even if asymptomatic)
- **Follow-up:**
    - Anticoagulation clinic INR check **within 2–5 days** (sooner if reversal given)
    - GP review if new symptoms or medication changes

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
    - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially tachycardia, hypotension)
    - Suspected intracranial bleed (urgent CT head needed)
    - Haemodynamically unstable major haemorrhage (systolic BP <90, HR >120)
    - Lactate >4 mmol/L
    - GCS drop by ≥2 points
    - INR >10 with active bleeding (or need for emergency surgery) — call haematology on-call for PCC dosing
- **Crash team:**
    - Cardiac arrest (consider haemorrhagic shock, tamponade, tension pneumothorax)
    - Seizure (possibility of intracranial bleed)
